essential_question: Why is multiplying by √2/√2 always legal, and why does it always clear the denominator?
objective: Justify why multiplying a fraction by √n/√n never changes its value, justify why the radical always cancels from the denominator, review the week's Math-U-See practice, and pass the mastery checkpoint.

content.prompt: Without looking anything up, rationalize 5/√3 and 8/√2 from scratch. Then check your work against your concept note and your measurement log from Session 2.

content.heading: Go over Lesson Practice 19A & 19B together
content.note: Bring your completed MUS Lesson Practice 19A/19B. We check the tricky ones: sums like 4/√5 + 2/√6 where each term must be rationalized BEFORE finding a common denominator, subtraction problems like 5/√10 − 3/√8 where √8 simplifies further, and the fill-in-the-blank vocabulary (radicals shouldn't be left in the denominator; multiplying numerator and denominator by the same number is really multiplying by one; find the common denominator before adding). Fix any you missed and note WHY.

content.heading: Defend three claims
bullets: Claim 1: multiplying 1/√2 by √2/√2 never changes its value. Argue it from the fact that √2/√2 equals 1, and multiplying any number by 1 leaves it unchanged.
Claim 2: multiplying a single-term radical denominator by itself ALWAYS produces a whole number, no exceptions. Argue it from the identity √n × √n = n.
Claim 3: 3/√2 and 5/√8 can be combined into one term, but 3/√2 and 5/√7 can never be combined, no matter how each is rationalized. Argue it from what happens to each radicand after rationalizing.

content.preview: Next week, Lesson 20 — Special Right Triangles — where 45-45-90 and 30-60-90 triangles hand you fractions like 10/√2 constantly, and rationalizing turns them into clean, exact answers.

success_criteria.text: I can justify why multiplying by √n/√n never changes a fraction's value and always clears the denominator
I can rationalize any single-radical denominator and simplify the result completely
I can justify when two rationalized fractions can combine and when they cannot

questions.explain: 6/√2 = 6√2/2, and 6 and 2 share a factor of 2, reducing to 3√2.

questions.prompt: Fill in the blank (straight from this week's Math-U-See practice): radicals should not be left in the ___ of a fraction.
questions.explain: A fraction is only fully simplified once the denominator is a whole number — the radical has to move to the numerator, or vanish, before the answer counts as finished.

questions.prompt: Combine: 4/√5 + 2/√20. Rationalize each term first, then find the common denominator.
questions.explain: √20 simplifies to 2√5, so 2/√20 rationalizes and reduces all the way to √5/5. Both terms now share denominator 5 and radicand 5, combining to 5√5/5 = √5.

questions.prompt: Rationalize 3/√8 first, then decide: can 3/√8 and 5/√2 be combined? If so, combine them.
questions.explain: 3/√8 looks unlike 5/√2 until you rationalize and simplify it — √8 reduces to 2√2, revealing the shared radicand 2 that lets the two fractions combine over a common denominator of 4.

questions.prompt: Which fact explains why multiplying 7/√3 by √3/√3 doesn't change its value?
questions.explain: Any nonzero number divided by itself equals 1, and multiplying by 1 never changes a number's value — that's the whole reason rationalizing is legal.

questions.prompt: In your own words, explain why multiplying the numerator and denominator of a fraction by √n over √n ALWAYS clears the radical from the denominator, no matter what whole number n is.
questions.answer: Because √n × √n = n for every whole number n — the two identical radicals multiply together to give a whole number every single time, while √n/√n = 1 guarantees the fraction's value never changes; the move is really just relabeling the same number, not changing it.
questions.explain: Full credit connects BOTH ideas: the identity √n × √n = n (why the radical disappears) and √n/√n = 1 (why the value stays the same).

questions.prompt: Close: rate yourself on Lesson 19 (Emerging / Proficient / Mastered) and name the one idea you'd want to revisit.
